refashioned:
Having discussed the virtues, it is natural to turn next to friendship, since friendship is itself a virtue or at least involves virtue, and is moreover one of the most essential things for human life. No one would choose to live without friends, even if they possessed every other good. The wealthy, the powerful, and those who hold office are thought to need friends most of all -- for what good is prosperity without the opportunity to do good for others, which is exercised above all toward friends? And how can prosperity be safeguarded without friends? The greater one's fortune, the more vulnerable it is. In poverty and misfortune, people regard friends as their only refuge. Friendship helps the young avoid mistakes; it supports older people by attending to their needs and compensating for their declining abilities; and it spurs those in their prime to noble action -- as Homer says, 'two going together' -- for with friends, people are better able both to think and to act.

Furthermore, the bond between parent and offspring seems to be planted by nature -- not only among humans but among birds and most animals. Members of the same species feel a natural affinity for one another, and this is especially strong among human beings, which is why we praise those who love their fellow humans. We can see even in our travels how every person feels a kinship with every other.

Friendship also seems to hold political communities together, and lawmakers care about it even more than about justice. For concord -- which is something like friendship -- is what they most strive to achieve, while they seek above all to drive out faction, which is friendship's greatest enemy. When people are friends, they have no need of justice; but when they are merely just, they still need friendship as well. Indeed, the highest form of justice is thought to have a friendly quality to it.
elegant:
Friendship is a virtue, or involves virtue, and is indispensable for a good life. No one would choose to live without friends, even possessing all other goods. The wealthy need friends to exercise generosity; the unfortunate need friends as refuge. Friendship helps the young avoid error, supports the old in weakness, and spurs the mature to noble action.

Nature implants this bond -- between parent and child, between members of the same kind, and especially among humans. Friendship holds states together; lawmakers prize it above justice itself. Concord resembles friendship, and faction is its enemy. When people are friends they need no justice, but when merely just they still need friendship. The truest justice has a friendly quality.
summary: Friendship is essential to human life at every stage and social position. It is natural, found across species, and politically vital -- lawmakers value it even above justice, since friends have no need of justice while just people still need friendship.
distilled: Friendship is indispensable to human flourishing, holding together both individual lives and political communities, and surpassing even justice in its binding power.
notes: The Homer quotation 'two going together' is from Iliad X.224, where Diomedes asks for a companion on a night raid. -- The claim that friendship is more important than justice to lawmakers anticipates Aristotle's extensive parallel treatment of friendship and justice throughout Book VIII, especially VIII.9 (8.22-8.23). -- 'Concord' translates homonoia, a key political concept Aristotle develops further in IX.6. -- The discussion of friendship holding states together connects directly to Politics III, where Aristotle treats civic friendship (politike philia) as essential to the polis.

refashioned:
Friendship is not only necessary but also noble, for we praise those who love their friends, and having many friends is considered admirable. Moreover, we tend to think that being a good person and being a good friend go together.

There is no shortage of debate about friendship. Some define it as a kind of likeness, claiming that like is drawn to like -- hence the proverbs 'like to like' and 'birds of a feather flock together.' Others take the opposite view, saying 'two of a trade never agree.' Some thinkers pursue deeper, more physical explanations: Euripides says that 'parched earth loves the rain, and stately heaven when filled with rain loves to fall to earth'; Heraclitus holds that 'it is what opposes that helps' and 'from different tones comes the fairest tune' and 'all things are produced through strife'; while Empedocles, along with others, argues the reverse -- that like aims at like.

The physical questions we may set aside, since they do not belong to the present inquiry. Let us instead examine those that concern human character and feeling: whether friendship can arise between any two people, or whether the wicked cannot be friends at all; and whether there is one kind of friendship or more than one. Those who maintain there is only one kind -- on the grounds that friendship admits of degrees -- rely on an insufficient indicator, for even things that differ in kind can admit of degree. This point we have addressed previously.
elegant: Friendship is not only necessary but noble; we praise those who love their friends and assume good people make good friends. The nature of friendship is debated: some say like attracts like, others that opposites attract. Euripides, Heraclitus, and Empedocles each offer physical explanations. But setting aside natural philosophy, the human questions are: can any two people be friends, or only the good? And is there one species of friendship or several? Those who claim only one kind, because friendship admits of degrees, reason poorly -- even things different in kind admit of degree.
summary: Friendship is both necessary and noble. Ancient thinkers debated whether like attracts like or opposites attract, but Aristotle sets aside these physical theories to focus on ethical questions: who can be friends, and how many kinds of friendship exist.
distilled: Beyond being necessary, friendship is noble -- and the key ethical questions are who can truly be friends and whether friendship comes in distinct kinds.
notes: Euripides (c. 480-406 BC): Athenian tragedian; the quotation is from a lost play, possibly the Chrysippus. -- Heraclitus (c. 535-475 BC): pre-Socratic philosopher of Ephesus, famous for the doctrine that conflict and opposition underlie all reality. -- Empedocles (c. 494-434 BC): pre-Socratic philosopher who taught that Love (philia) and Strife are the two cosmic forces; like elements are drawn together by Love. -- 'We have discussed this matter previously' likely refers to the discussion of degrees within genera in the Categories or to ethical discussions earlier in the Ethics. -- The debate between like-attracts-like and opposites-attract was a major theme in pre-Socratic natural philosophy, which Aristotle here deliberately brackets to keep the inquiry ethical.

refashioned:
The kinds of friendship may become clearer if we first identify what is lovable. Not everything is loved -- only what is lovable, and the lovable is what is good, pleasant, or useful. But the useful appears to be that which produces some good or pleasure, so that strictly speaking only the good and the pleasant are lovable as ends.

Do people love what is truly good, or what is good for them? These sometimes conflict -- and so too with the pleasant. Each person is thought to love what is good for himself, and while the good is lovable without qualification, what is lovable for each person is what is good for him specifically. In practice, each person loves not what is actually good for him but what appears good. This need not trouble us; we simply note that 'the lovable' means 'what seems lovable.'

There are, then, three grounds for loving. We do not speak of 'friendship' toward lifeless objects, since there is no mutual affection involved, nor any wishing of good for the object's own sake. It would be absurd to wish wine well -- if we wish anything for it, it is that it keep so we can enjoy it. But we say we should wish good to a friend for his own sake. Those who wish good to another without reciprocation we credit only with goodwill; goodwill that is mutual constitutes friendship.

Or must we add a further condition -- that the goodwill be recognized? Many people bear goodwill toward those they have never met but judge to be good or useful, and the feeling may well be returned. Such people seem to have goodwill toward each other, yet how can we call them friends when neither knows of the other's feelings? To be friends, then, people must have mutually recognized goodwill and wish each other well for one of the reasons mentioned above.
elegant:
To understand friendship, we must first identify what is lovable. Only the good, the pleasant, and the useful are lovable -- though the useful is really just what produces good or pleasure. Each person loves what seems good to him, giving us three grounds for love.

We do not speak of friendship toward lifeless things, since there is no mutual affection and no wishing good for the object's sake. Wishing good to someone without return is mere goodwill. When goodwill is mutual, it begins to resemble friendship -- but true friendship requires that each party recognize the other's goodwill. People who bear goodwill to strangers they have never met cannot be called friends. Friendship, then, requires mutually recognized goodwill grounded in goodness, pleasure, or utility.
summary: There are three reasons people love: goodness, pleasure, and utility. Friendship differs from mere goodwill in requiring mutual and recognized well-wishing between the parties, not just one-sided feeling toward someone or fondness for things.
distilled: Friendship requires mutually recognized goodwill between people who wish each other well for the sake of goodness, pleasure, or utility.
notes: This passage establishes the tripartite division of the lovable (good, pleasant, useful) that structures the entire friendship discussion through Books VIII-IX. -- The distinction between what is 'good without qualification' and 'good for a particular person' echoes discussions in Book I and Book VII. -- The wine example (wishing wine well) is one of Aristotle's characteristic reductio illustrations. -- The requirement that goodwill be 'recognized' (gnorizomenon) adds an epistemic condition beyond mere feeling, distinguishing Aristotle's account from purely affective theories of friendship.

refashioned:
Since these three reasons for loving differ in kind, so do the corresponding forms of love and friendship. There are therefore three kinds of friendship, matching the three things that are lovable. In each case, the love is mutual and recognized, and those who love each other wish each other well in the respect in which they love.

Those who love each other for utility do not love each other for who they are, but for the benefit each derives from the other. The same goes for those who love for the sake of pleasure: people enjoy the company of the witty not because of their character, but because they find them amusing. Thus those who love for utility love for the sake of what is good for themselves, and those who love for pleasure love for the sake of what is pleasant to themselves -- not insofar as the loved person is who he is, but insofar as he is useful or pleasant.

These friendships are therefore incidental: the person is loved not as the individual he is, but as someone who provides a certain good or pleasure. Such friendships dissolve easily, since if one party ceases to be pleasant or useful, the other ceases to love him.
elegant: The three grounds for loving produce three kinds of friendship. Those who love for utility love not the person but the benefit; those who love for pleasure love not the character but the enjoyment. In both cases the person is loved incidentally -- not for who he is, but for what he provides. These friendships dissolve easily when one party ceases to be useful or pleasant.
summary: The three reasons for loving -- goodness, pleasure, and utility -- produce three corresponding kinds of friendship. Friendships based on utility or pleasure are incidental, since the person is valued not for who they are but for what they provide, and they dissolve when the benefit ends.
distilled: Friendships based on utility or pleasure are incidental and fragile, since the person is loved not for themselves but for the benefit they provide.
notes: The term 'incidental' (kata sumbebekos) is a technical Aristotelian term meaning the friendship attaches to an accidental rather than essential quality of the person. -- This tripartite classification of friendship becomes one of the most influential frameworks in the history of ethics, widely discussed by Thomas Aquinas, Montaigne, and modern virtue ethicists. -- The argument that utility and pleasure friendships are 'not for the person himself' prefigures the Kantian distinction between treating persons as ends versus means, though Aristotle's framework differs significantly.

refashioned:
The useful is not permanent but constantly changing. So when the reason for the friendship disappears, the friendship dissolves with it, since it existed only for those ends. This kind of friendship seems to arise chiefly among the elderly, who at that age pursue the useful rather than the pleasant, and among those in their prime or youth who are focused on practical advantage. Such people do not even spend much time together -- sometimes they do not find each other pleasant -- and so they have no need for regular companionship unless they are useful to each other. They find each other agreeable only insofar as they kindle hopes of some future benefit. The friendship between host and guest is also placed in this category.

The friendship of the young, by contrast, seems to aim at pleasure. Young people live under the sway of emotion and pursue above all what is immediately pleasant. But as they grow older their pleasures change, so they become friends quickly and fall out of friendship just as quickly -- their friendship shifts along with whatever they find pleasant, and such pleasures change rapidly. Young people are also prone to romantic love, since much of erotic friendship depends on emotion and aims at pleasure. This is why they fall in love and out of love so easily, often changing within a single day. Yet they do wish to spend their days and lives together, for that is how they fulfill the purpose of their friendship.
elegant:
The useful is ever-changing, so utility friendships dissolve when the benefit ends. These arise chiefly among the old, who seek advantage over pleasure, and among the practically minded at any age. Such friends need not even enjoy each other's company.

Young people's friendships aim at pleasure. Living by emotion, they pursue what is immediately delightful -- but since their pleasures shift rapidly, they become friends quickly and part just as quickly. Romantic love belongs here too: driven by feeling and aimed at pleasure, it flares up and dies down within a day. Yet these friends desire to spend their lives together, which is the very purpose of their friendship.
summary: Utility friendships are common among the elderly and dissolve when the advantage ends. Pleasure friendships are common among the young, who form and break bonds rapidly as their emotions shift. Romantic love, driven by feeling and aimed at pleasure, falls into this volatile category.
distilled: Utility friendships belong to the old and dissolve with changing circumstances; pleasure friendships belong to the young and shift with changing emotions.
notes: The friendship of host and guest (xenia) was a sacred institution in ancient Greece, governed by Zeus Xenios, involving reciprocal obligations of hospitality. -- Aristotle's association of youth with emotion-driven friendship reflects his broader developmental psychology, in which reason increasingly governs as one matures (see also Rhetoric II.12-14 on the characters of different ages). -- The observation that lovers 'change often within a single day' reflects Greek literary treatments of eros, especially in comedy and lyric poetry.

refashioned:
Complete friendship is the friendship of people who are good and alike in virtue. Such friends wish each other well as good people, and they are good in themselves. Those who wish well to their friends for the friends' own sake are friends in the truest sense, for they feel this way because of their own character, not for any incidental reason. Their friendship therefore lasts as long as they remain good -- and goodness is an enduring quality.

Moreover, each is good both without qualification and to his friend, since the good are both genuinely good and mutually useful. They are also pleasant to each other, since good people take pleasure both in their own activities and in similar activities in others, and the actions of the good are the same or alike.

Such friendship is, as one would expect, permanent, because it combines all the qualities friends should have. Every friendship is for the sake of the good or the pleasant -- whether in the abstract or as experienced by the one who feels the affection -- and is grounded in a certain likeness. In the friendship of good people, all these qualities belong to the friends by virtue of their own nature: goodness, usefulness, pleasantness, and likeness are all present. What is good without qualification is also pleasant without qualification, and these are the most lovable qualities of all. Love and friendship are therefore found at their fullest and best between such people.
elegant: Complete friendship exists between people who are good and alike in virtue. They wish each other well for the other's own sake, motivated by their own character rather than any incidental benefit. Since goodness endures, so does their friendship. Each is genuinely good, useful to the other, and pleasant -- since the good take pleasure in good activity, whether their own or their friend's. This friendship unites all the qualities that friendship requires: goodness, utility, pleasure, and likeness. What is unconditionally good is also unconditionally pleasant, making such friends the most lovable. Love and friendship reach their highest form between such people.
summary: The highest friendship exists between virtuous people who love each other for their own sake. This friendship is permanent because goodness endures, and it naturally combines all three lovable qualities -- goodness, utility, and pleasure -- making it the most complete form of human bond.
distilled: Perfect friendship exists between good people who love each other for their character itself, uniting goodness, pleasure, and utility in an enduring bond.
notes: This is the central passage defining Aristotle's concept of teleia philia (complete/perfect friendship), one of the most discussed concepts in the history of ethics. -- The argument that goodness is 'enduring' (monimon) contrasts with the instability of pleasure and utility discussed in the previous sections. -- The claim that the good are 'pleasant to each other' connects to Book X's discussion of pleasure as completing virtuous activity. -- The friendship of virtue has influenced Christian theological discussions of caritas, Aquinas's treatment of friendship with God, and modern virtue ethics (e.g., Alasdair MacIntyre, Martha Nussbaum).

refashioned:
But it is natural that such friendships should be rare, because such people are rare. Furthermore, this kind of friendship requires time and familiarity. As the proverb says, people cannot know each other until they have 'eaten salt together.' They cannot accept each other into friendship or be friends until each has shown himself to be lovable and has earned the other's trust.

Those who quickly display the signs of friendship to each other wish to be friends, but they are not yet friends unless both are truly lovable and each recognizes this in the other. A wish for friendship can arise quickly, but friendship itself cannot.
elegant: Such friendships are naturally rare because virtuous people are rare. They also require time and familiarity -- as the proverb says, you must 'eat salt together' before you can know someone. Those who quickly show signs of friendship wish to be friends but are not yet friends until each has proven lovable and trustworthy. The desire for friendship arises quickly; friendship itself does not.
summary: Perfect friendships are rare because virtuous people are rare, and even between good people friendship requires extended time and familiarity to develop. Quick affinity signals a wish for friendship, not friendship itself.
distilled: True friendship is rare and slow to form, requiring time, familiarity, and mutual proof of character.
notes: The proverb about 'eating salt together' (hals sunanalōsai) was widespread in the ancient Mediterranean world; salt was a symbol of shared meals and lasting bonds. -- The distinction between wishing for friendship (boulēsis philias) and actual friendship reinforces Aristotle's insistence on the conditions of mutuality and recognition established in 8.3. -- This passage is often cited in discussions of whether Aristotelian friendship is compatible with modern social conditions and the pace of contemporary life.

refashioned:
This kind of friendship, then, is complete both in duration and in every other respect. In it, each friend receives from the other the same as -- or something equivalent to -- what he gives, which is exactly what should happen between friends.

Friendship for pleasure bears a resemblance to this complete kind, since good people are also pleasant to each other. Friendship for utility is similar too, since the good are also useful to each other. Even among people of lesser character, friendships are most lasting when the friends receive the same thing from each other -- for example, pleasure -- and from the same source, as happens between witty people. This differs from the case of lover and beloved, who do not take pleasure in the same things: the lover takes pleasure in seeing the beloved, while the beloved takes pleasure in receiving the lover's attention. When the bloom of youth fades, the friendship sometimes fades with it -- the lover no longer finds pleasure in the sight, and the beloved no longer receives attention. Yet many lovers do remain constant, provided that through familiarity they have come to love each other's character, their characters being alike.

Those who exchange utility rather than pleasure in their romantic relationships are both less truly friends and less enduring. Those who are friends for the sake of utility part when the advantage comes to an end, for they were lovers not of each other but of profit.
elegant: Complete friendship is perfect in duration and every other respect: each friend gives and receives equally. Pleasure friendships resemble it, since the good are also pleasant; utility friendships do too, since the good are also useful. Among lesser friendships, the most stable are those where both parties receive the same thing from the same source. Lover and beloved often enjoy different things -- one the sight, the other the attention -- so the bond may dissolve when youth fades. But lovers who come to love each other's character through familiarity can endure. Those who exchange utility in romance are least lasting, for they loved profit, not each other.
summary: Complete friendship involves equal exchange and is perfect in every respect. Pleasure and utility friendships resemble it partially. Romantic relationships are most enduring when partners come to love each other's character; those based purely on utility dissolve when the advantage ends.
distilled: The most lasting bonds -- whether friendships or romantic relationships -- are those where people come to love each other's character, not merely what each provides.
notes: The observation about lover and beloved not taking pleasure in the same things reflects the Greek cultural context of erotic relationships (erōs), particularly the asymmetric pederastic model common in Aristotle's Athens. -- The claim that lovers can transition to character-based friendship through familiarity (sunētheia) is notable as an early recognition that romantic love can mature into deeper friendship. -- This passage is continued and deepened in Book IX's discussion of what friends owe each other.

refashioned: For the sake of pleasure or utility, then, even bad people may be friends with each other, and good people may be friends with bad, and someone who is neither good nor bad may befriend anyone. But for their own sake -- that is, for who they truly are -- clearly only good people can be friends. Bad people take no delight in each other unless some advantage comes from the relationship.
elegant: Pleasure and utility friendships can exist between any combination of people, including the bad. But friendship based on who people truly are can exist only between the good, since bad people find no delight in each other apart from some advantage.
summary: Friendships of pleasure or utility can form between anyone, regardless of character. But only good people can be friends for each other's own sake, since the bad find no inherent delight in one another.
distilled: Only the good can be friends in the deepest sense -- for each other's own sake rather than for any benefit.
notes: This brief passage summarizes the asymmetry at the heart of Aristotle's friendship theory: the lower forms of friendship are open to all, but the highest form has a moral prerequisite. -- The claim that bad people 'do not delight in each other' connects to Aristotle's analysis of the vicious person's inner conflict in IX.4, where he argues that the wicked are at odds even with themselves.

refashioned:
The friendship of the good is also uniquely resistant to slander. It is not easy to believe someone else's accusations against a person you have tested yourself over a long time. Between good friends there exists trust, the conviction that 'he would never wrong me,' and all the other marks of true friendship. In the other kinds of friendship, however, nothing prevents such problems from arising.

People do apply the word 'friends' to those whose bond is based on utility -- in this sense, even states are called 'friendly,' since alliances between states aim at mutual advantage. And people also call those who love each other for pleasure 'friends,' as when children are described that way. We too should perhaps use the term for such relationships and say that there are several kinds of friendship: first and in the proper sense, the friendship of good people as good, and then, by analogy, the other kinds. For it is in virtue of something good -- something resembling what is found in true friendship -- that they are friends, since even pleasure is a good for those who love pleasure. But these two inferior kinds are not often combined in the same relationship, nor do the same people typically become friends for both utility and pleasure, since things only incidentally connected are rarely found together.

With friendship divided in this way, bad people will be friends for the sake of pleasure or utility, being alike in that respect, while good people will be friends for each other's own sake -- that is, by virtue of their goodness. The latter are friends without qualification; the former are friends only incidentally, through their resemblance to true friendship.
elegant: The friendship of the good alone withstands slander, built on long-tested trust and the certainty that the friend would never do wrong. Other friendships have no such protection. We do call utility bonds 'friendship' -- even between states -- and pleasure bonds too, as among children. There are therefore several kinds: friendship of the good in the proper sense, and others by analogy, since they contain something good that resembles true friendship. But utility and pleasure friendships rarely combine, since incidental connections seldom coincide. Bad people befriend each other for pleasure or utility; good people befriend each other for their own sake. The latter are friends without qualification; the former only incidentally.
summary: True friendship between good people is uniquely resistant to slander because it rests on long-tested trust. While we legitimately call utility and pleasure bonds 'friendships,' these are friendships only by analogy. Good people are friends without qualification; others are friends incidentally.
distilled: Only the friendship of the good withstands slander and merits the name 'friendship' without qualification -- all other bonds are friendships only by analogy.
notes: The idea that states can be 'friends' (philai) in the utility sense connects to the discussion of political friendship in VIII.9 and to Aristotle's analysis of interstate alliances in the Politics. -- The distinction between friendship 'in the proper sense' (kuriōs) and 'by analogy' (kath' homoiotēta) is a characteristic Aristotelian logical move, paralleling his treatment of terms like 'being' and 'good' as said in many ways (pollachōs legomenon). -- The passage establishes the focal meaning analysis of friendship that Thomas Aquinas later adopted for his account of analogical predication.

refashioned:
Just as some people are called good with respect to a settled character trait and others with respect to their actual activity, so too with friendship. Those who live together take active delight in each other and do good for each other, while those who are asleep or geographically separated are not performing the activities of friendship but remain disposed to perform them. Distance does not destroy friendship outright but only suspends its activity. If the absence is prolonged, however, it does seem to cause people to forget their friendship -- hence the saying 'out of sight, out of mind.'

Neither old people nor sour people seem to make friends easily, for there is little in them that is pleasant, and no one can spend his days with someone whose company is painful or even merely unpleasant. Nature above all seeks to avoid the painful and aims at the pleasant. Those who approve of each other but do not live together seem more like well-disposed acquaintances than actual friends. Nothing is so characteristic of friendship as living together. Even those who are not in need desire the company of others, and the supremely happy desire it most of all, since solitude suits such people least. But people cannot live together unless they are pleasant to each other and enjoy the same things, as companions in friendship seem to do.
elegant: As virtue exists both as disposition and as activity, so does friendship. Friends who live together actively delight in and benefit each other; separated friends retain the disposition but not the activity. Prolonged absence can even cause forgetfulness -- 'out of sight, out of mind.' The old and the sour make friends with difficulty, since they offer little pleasure, and nature avoids the painful. Those who approve of each other without living together are well-disposed but not truly friends. Living together is friendship's defining feature -- even the supremely happy need companionship, for solitude suits them least. But shared life requires being pleasant to each other and enjoying the same things.
summary: Friendship exists both as a disposition and as an active practice. Living together is its most characteristic feature, and prolonged separation can erode it. Since companionship requires mutual pleasantness, the old and the sour struggle to form new friendships.
distilled: Friendship lives in shared daily life, and its essential activity -- not merely the disposition -- requires that friends find genuine pleasure in each other's company.
notes: The distinction between hexis (state/disposition) and energeia (activity) is fundamental to Aristotle's metaphysics and psychology, applied here to friendship as it is to virtue in Book II. -- 'Out of sight, out of mind' translates a Greek proverb; Aristotle treats proverbs as condensed practical wisdom throughout his works. -- The claim that even the supremely happy need companionship challenges any ideal of self-sufficiency as solitude, connecting to the discussion of self-sufficiency in Book I and to IX.9's argument that the happy person needs friends.

refashioned:
The truest friendship, then, is that of the good, as we have said repeatedly. What is good or pleasant without qualification seems to be lovable and desirable, and for each person, what is good or pleasant to him. The good person is lovable and desirable to another good person for both these reasons.

It appears that love is a feeling, while friendship is a settled state of character. Love can be felt even toward lifeless things, but mutual love involves deliberate choice, and choice springs from a state of character. When people wish well to those they love, they do so for the loved one's sake -- not as a result of mere feeling but from a settled disposition.

In loving a friend, a person loves what is good for himself, for the good person, in becoming a friend, becomes a good to his friend. Each person both loves what is good for himself and returns an equal measure of goodwill and pleasantness. Friendship is said to be equality, and both goodwill and pleasantness are found most fully in the friendship of the good.
elegant: The truest friendship belongs to the good. Love is a feeling; friendship is a state of character. Love can be directed at things, but mutual love requires deliberate choice rooted in character. Friends wish well to each other for the other's own sake -- not from mere emotion but from settled disposition. In loving a good friend, one loves what is genuinely good for oneself, since the good person becomes a good to his friend. Each loves what benefits himself and returns equal goodwill and pleasure. Friendship is equality, most fully realized between the good.
summary: Love is a feeling, but friendship is a deliberate state of character involving choice. In the friendship of good people, each person becomes a genuine good to the other, creating an equality of goodwill and mutual benefit that constitutes friendship at its truest.
distilled: Friendship differs from love as a state of character differs from a feeling -- it requires deliberate choice and creates a mutual equality of goodwill between the good.
notes: The distinction between pathos (feeling/passion) and hexis (state of character) is central to Aristotle's moral psychology throughout the Ethics, especially in Book II's account of virtue. -- The claim that 'friendship is equality' (isotēs hē philia) became proverbial in antiquity and is discussed extensively in VIII.7 regarding unequal friendships. -- The argument that in loving a friend one loves 'what is good for oneself' does not reduce friendship to self-interest; rather, it reflects Aristotle's view that the good person's self-love is virtuous (developed in IX.8).

refashioned:
Between sour and elderly people, friendship arises less readily, since they are less good-tempered and less inclined to enjoy companionship -- qualities thought to be the greatest marks of friendship and what produces it. This is why young people become friends quickly while old people do not: people do not become friends with those in whom they find no delight. Similarly, sour people do not make friends quickly either.

Such people may, however, bear goodwill toward each other, for they wish one another well and help each other in need. But they are scarcely friends in the full sense, because they do not spend their days together or take delight in each other -- and these are considered the greatest marks of friendship.
elegant: The elderly and the sour make friends with difficulty because they lack good temper and enjoyment of company -- the chief marks of friendship. Young people befriend each other readily; the old and sour do not. Such people may bear goodwill and offer mutual aid, but without spending time together and delighting in each other's company, they fall short of true friendship.
summary: Elderly and ill-tempered people struggle to form friendships because they lack the pleasantness and sociability that friendship requires. They may wish each other well, but goodwill alone, without shared enjoyment, does not constitute real friendship.
distilled: Goodwill without shared enjoyment falls short of friendship, which is why the sour and elderly -- lacking pleasantness -- struggle to form true bonds.
notes: This passage reinforces the emphasis on living together (suzēn) and mutual delight as constitutive of friendship, not merely as incidental features. -- The repeated contrast between goodwill (eunoia) and friendship proper highlights Aristotle's view that friendship requires active, ongoing engagement. -- The characterization of the elderly here should be read alongside Rhetoric II.13, where Aristotle gives a more nuanced portrait of old age.

refashioned:
One cannot have the perfect kind of friendship with many people, just as one cannot be romantically in love with many people at once. Love is a kind of excess of feeling, and such intensity is by its nature directed toward one person. It is difficult for many people simultaneously to be greatly pleasing to the same individual, or perhaps even to be genuinely good in his eyes. Moreover, one must gain experience of the other person and become familiar with him, which is very hard to do with many.

But when it comes to utility or pleasure, it is quite possible for many people to be pleasing to one person, since many people can be useful or pleasant, and these interactions take little time.
elegant: One cannot have perfect friendship with many, just as one cannot be in love with many at once -- such intensity is naturally directed at one person. It is hard for many to be simultaneously pleasing or genuinely good in one person's eyes, and the familiarity required takes great effort. But many can please through utility or pleasure, since these require little time.
summary: Perfect friendship, like romantic love, is necessarily limited to a few because of its intensity and the deep familiarity it demands. Utility and pleasure friendships can be more numerous since they require less investment.
distilled: Perfect friendship is necessarily rare and exclusive, limited by the intensity of feeling and depth of familiarity it demands.
notes: The comparison of perfect friendship to erotic love (erōs) in terms of exclusivity is striking and connects to Plato's Symposium and Phaedrus, where eros is treated as directed toward a single beloved. -- The practical limitation on the number of close friends has been echoed in modern social science, notably Robin Dunbar's research on cognitive limits to the number of stable relationships (Dunbar's number). -- This passage anticipates the fuller discussion in IX.10 of how many friends one should have.

refashioned:
Of these two kinds, the friendship based on pleasure is more like true friendship, especially when both parties receive the same things from each other and delight in each other or in the same activities, as in the friendships of the young. There is more generosity in such friendships. Friendship based on utility, by contrast, belongs to the commercially minded.

Even the supremely happy have no need of useful friends, but they do need pleasant companions, for they wish to live with others. Though they can endure what is painful for a short time, no one could bear it continuously -- not even the Good itself, if it were painful to experience. This is why they seek out friends who are pleasant. But perhaps they should also seek friends who are good, and good for them specifically, so that their friendships possess all the qualities that friends should have.
elegant: Pleasure friendship resembles true friendship more than utility friendship does, especially when both parties enjoy the same things -- as the young do. Such friendships contain more generosity; utility friendships suit the commercially minded. Even the supremely happy need pleasant friends, since they wish for companionship and no one can endure the painful indefinitely. Ideally, they should seek friends who are pleasant, good, and good for them -- combining all the qualities friendship requires.
summary: Pleasure friendships resemble true friendship more than utility friendships do, being more generous and reciprocal. Even the happiest people need pleasant companions, though they should ideally seek friends who are also good.
distilled: Even the supremely happy need pleasant friends for companionship, but the ideal is to find friends who are pleasant, good, and good for you.
notes: The remark that no one could endure even 'the Good itself' if it were painful is a pointed philosophical claim, possibly directed at Plato's Form of the Good (Republic VI-VII), suggesting that abstract goodness without experiential pleasantness is insufficient for human life. -- The 'commercially minded' (agoraioi) is a term with social connotations in ancient Greece, referring to those who frequent the marketplace. -- The ideal of combining all three qualities in a friend connects to the discussion of the complete good in Book I.

refashioned:
People in positions of power seem to have friends who fall into distinct groups: some are useful to them and others are pleasant, but rarely are the same people both. They do not seek those whose pleasantness is accompanied by virtue, nor those whose usefulness serves noble ends. Instead, in their desire for amusement they seek witty people, and for their other needs they choose people who are clever at carrying out instructions -- and these qualities are seldom found together.

Now we have said that the good person is at the same time pleasant and useful. But such a person does not become the friend of someone who surpasses him in social position, unless that superior also surpasses him in virtue. If this condition is not met, the inferior cannot establish the proportional equality needed. But people who surpass others in both power and virtue are not easy to find.
elegant: The powerful tend to separate their friends into the useful and the pleasant, rarely finding both in one person. They seek entertainers for pleasure and competent subordinates for utility -- neither selected for virtue. The good person combines pleasantness and usefulness but will not befriend a social superior unless that superior also excels in virtue, establishing proportional equality. People superior in both station and virtue are rare.
summary: Powerful people typically divide their friends into those who are entertaining and those who are useful, selecting neither for virtue. A truly good person will only befriend a social superior who also surpasses them in virtue, but such people are rare.
distilled: The powerful fragment friendship into pleasure and utility, rarely finding both in one person -- and the good person will not befriend a superior who lacks proportional virtue.
notes: The portrait of the powerful person's fragmented friendships reflects Greek political life, where tyrants and rulers surrounded themselves with flatterers and functionaries. -- The concept of proportional equality in unequal friendships connects to the broader discussion of proportional justice in Book V. -- The 'ready-witted' (eutrapeloi) are discussed more fully in Book IV.8, where Aristotle treats wit as a social virtue -- the mean between buffoonery and boorishness.

refashioned:
However that may be, the friendships we have been discussing involve equality: the friends receive the same things from each other and wish the same things for each other, or they exchange one thing for another -- for example, pleasure for utility. We have said, however, that these friendships are both less genuine and less enduring than the friendship of virtue.

It is because of their likeness and unlikeness to the friendship of virtue that they seem both to be and not to be true friendships. On one hand, they resemble the friendship of virtue -- one involves pleasure and the other utility, and both pleasure and utility are also present in the friendship of virtue. On the other hand, because the friendship of virtue resists slander and is permanent while these other kinds change quickly and differ from it in many other ways, they seem not to be genuine friendships at all. In short, it is their unlikeness to the friendship of virtue that makes them appear to fall short.
elegant: The friendships discussed involve equality -- receiving the same things or exchanging one kind of good for another. But they are less genuine and less lasting than virtue friendship. They both resemble and differ from it: they share its elements of pleasure or utility, but they lack its resistance to slander and its permanence. It is their unlikeness to virtue friendship that makes them seem not to be true friendships.
summary: Utility and pleasure friendships involve a kind of equality but are less genuine and less enduring than virtue friendship. They resemble it in containing pleasure or utility but differ in lacking permanence and resilience, which is why they seem to fall short of true friendship.
distilled: Pleasure and utility friendships both resemble and fall short of virtue friendship -- sharing some of its qualities while lacking its permanence and resilience.
notes: This passage exhibits Aristotle's characteristic method of explaining why common opinions (endoxa) are partially correct: pleasure and utility friendships are genuine enough to earn the name, but only by analogy with the primary case. -- The concept of friendship 'admitting of more and less' while differing in kind was introduced in 8.2 and is here resolved. -- The transition prepares for the discussion of unequal friendships in the next chapter.

refashioned:
There is another kind of friendship that involves inequality between the parties -- for example, the friendship of father and son, of elder and younger, of husband and wife, and in general of ruler and subject. These friendships also differ from one another: the bond between parent and child is not the same as that between ruler and subject, nor is the father's friendship toward his son the same as the son's toward his father, nor the husband's toward his wife the same as hers toward him. Each party has a different virtue and function, and the reasons for which they love differ. The love, and therefore the friendship, is different on each side.

Accordingly, each party neither receives the same from the other nor should seek the same. But when children give to parents what is owed to those who brought them into the world, and parents give to children what they should, the friendship between them will be lasting and excellent.

In all friendships that involve inequality, the love should be proportional: the better party should be loved more than he loves, as should the more useful, and similarly in every other case. When the love is proportional to the merit of each party, a kind of equality arises -- and equality is held to be the very essence of friendship.
elegant:
Friendship also exists between unequals -- father and son, husband and wife, ruler and subject. These differ from each other: each party has a different virtue, function, and reason for loving, so the friendship is different on each side. Neither party receives nor should seek the same thing from the other. When each renders what is appropriate, the friendship endures.

In all unequal friendships, love should be proportional to merit: the better or more useful party should receive more love. When love matches merit proportionally, a kind of equality emerges -- and equality is the essence of friendship.
summary: Friendships between unequals -- parent and child, husband and wife, ruler and subject -- involve different virtues, functions, and reasons for loving on each side. These friendships endure when each party fulfills their appropriate role, and love is proportional to merit, creating a form of equality.
distilled: In unequal friendships, proportional love -- where the better party is loved more -- creates the equality that friendship essentially requires.
notes: The treatment of husband-wife friendship as involving different virtues and functions reflects Aristotle's views in Politics I.12-13, where he argues men and women possess different forms of virtue suited to their roles. -- The principle that love should be proportional to merit parallels the discussion of distributive justice in Book V.3. -- The idea that unequal friendships can achieve equality through proportional love is one of Aristotle's most original contributions to the theory of friendship, later developed by Aquinas in his discussion of charity between God and humans.

refashioned:
But equality does not take the same form in friendship as it does in justice. In justice, what is primarily equal is what corresponds to merit -- proportional equality -- while quantitative equality is secondary. In friendship, it is the reverse: quantitative equality comes first, and proportion to merit is secondary.

This becomes clear when there is a great gap between the parties in virtue, vice, wealth, or anything else -- for then they are no longer friends and do not even expect to be. This is most obvious with the gods, who surpass us decisively in every good thing. But it is evident with kings too: those who are far beneath them do not expect to be their friends, nor do people of no standing expect to be friends with the best or wisest.

In such cases it is impossible to define precisely where friendship ceases to be possible. Much can be stripped away and friendship can survive, but when one party is separated by a vast distance -- as God is from mortals -- friendship becomes impossible. This gives rise to the question whether friends truly wish the greatest goods for their friends, such as that they become gods. For in that case they would no longer be friends, and therefore would no longer be goods for them, since friends are among our goods.

The answer is this: if we were right to say that a friend wishes good to his friend for the friend's sake, then the friend must remain the kind of being he is. One wishes the greatest goods for him only insofar as he remains a human being. And perhaps not even all the greatest goods without limit, for each person wishes what is good most of all for himself.
elegant:
Equality works differently in friendship and justice. In justice, proportional equality (according to merit) is primary; in friendship, quantitative equality comes first. When the gap between parties in virtue or standing is too great, friendship becomes impossible -- as between mortals and gods, or commoners and kings.

This raises a puzzle: should we wish our friends the greatest goods, even to become gods? No -- for then they would cease to be our friends. A friend wishes good to his friend for the friend's sake, but only while the friend remains the kind of being he is. We wish the greatest goods for friends insofar as they remain human. And each person ultimately wishes what is good most of all for himself.
summary: Friendship prioritizes quantitative equality while justice prioritizes proportional equality. When the gap between parties becomes too great, friendship is impossible. We wish friends the greatest goods only insofar as they remain who they are -- wishing them to become gods would destroy the friendship itself.
distilled: Friendship requires a certain equality between the parties and cannot survive an unbridgeable gap -- we wish friends the greatest goods only insofar as they remain the kind of beings they are.
notes: The contrast between equality in friendship and justice directly connects to Book V's treatment of distributive justice. -- The puzzle about wishing friends to become gods was widely discussed in ancient philosophy; it touches on the tension between self-love and altruism in friendship. -- The observation that 'each person wishes what is good most of all for himself' is developed extensively in IX.8, where Aristotle defends a form of virtuous self-love. -- The impossibility of friendship with God due to the distance between divine and human nature influenced later theological debates, particularly in Aquinas's question of whether humans can be friends with God (Summa Theologiae II-II, q.23).

refashioned:
Most people seem, because of ambition, to prefer being loved to loving. This is why most people enjoy flattery -- for the flatterer is a friend in an inferior position, or at least pretends to be, appearing to love more than he is loved. Being loved seems akin to being honored, and that is what most people pursue.

But it appears that people do not choose honor for its own sake, but only incidentally. Most people enjoy being honored by those in positions of authority because of what they hope to gain -- they believe that if they need something, they will get it from such people, and so they delight in honor as a sign of favors to come. Those who seek honor from good and knowledgeable people, on the other hand, aim to confirm their own opinion of themselves; they delight in honor because it reinforces their belief in their own goodness through the judgment of those who speak about them.

Being loved, by contrast, people enjoy for its own sake. This suggests that being loved is better than being honored, and that friendship is desirable in itself.

Yet friendship seems to consist more in loving than in being loved. This is shown by the delight mothers take in loving their children. Some mothers give their children to others to raise, and as long as they know their children's fate, they continue to love them without seeking to be loved in return -- when they cannot have both. They seem satisfied if they see their children thriving, and they love their children even when the children, out of ignorance, give nothing that a mother is due.

Since friendship depends more on loving, and since those who love their friends are praised, loving seems to be the characteristic excellence of friends. It is those in whom this quality is found in proper measure who are lasting friends, and whose friendship endures.
elegant:
Most people prefer being loved to loving, which is why they enjoy flattery -- the flatterer feigns loving more than he is loved. Being loved resembles being honored, which most people seek not for itself but for expected advantages or self-confirmation.

But being loved is valued for its own sake, suggesting it surpasses honor. Yet friendship consists more in loving than in being loved. Mothers demonstrate this: they love their children selflessly, giving them up to be raised by others yet continuing to love without expecting love in return, satisfied simply to see them thrive.

Since friendship depends more on loving, and lovers of friends are praised, loving is the characteristic excellence of friendship. Those who love properly are lasting friends.
summary: Most people prefer being loved over loving, but friendship actually consists more in loving. Mothers illustrate this perfectly: they love selflessly, seeking no return. Since the excellence of friendship lies in the act of loving, those who love well are the truest and most enduring friends.
distilled: The essence of friendship lies in loving, not in being loved -- as mothers show by loving selflessly without seeking anything in return.
notes: The analysis of flattery connects to Book IV.6, where the flatterer is one extreme of the virtue of friendliness (philia as a social virtue distinct from friendship proper). -- The argument that honor is sought instrumentally anticipates the discussion in Book I.5, where Aristotle rejects honor as the highest good because it depends on others. -- The mother example is one of Aristotle's most powerful illustrations, showing that the purest form of love expects no reciprocity. This challenges the emphasis on mutuality in his formal definition while enriching the account. -- The claim that loving is the 'characteristic virtue' (aretē) of friends treats friendship itself as admitting of excellence and deficiency.

refashioned:
It is in this way, more than any other, that even unequals can be friends: they can be equalized through proportional love. Equality and likeness constitute friendship, especially the likeness of those who are alike in virtue. Being steadfast in themselves, they hold fast to each other, neither asking for nor providing base services -- indeed, one might say they actively prevent them. It is characteristic of good people neither to go wrong themselves nor to let their friends do so.

Wicked people, by contrast, have no steadfastness, for they do not remain consistent even with themselves. They become friends only briefly, taking delight in each other's wickedness. Friends based on utility or pleasure last longer -- as long as they continue to provide each other with enjoyment or advantage.

Friendship for utility seems most naturally to arise between contraries -- between poor and rich, ignorant and learned -- since each seeks what he lacks and gives something else in return. Lover and beloved, beautiful and plain might also fall under this heading.

This is why lovers sometimes appear ridiculous when they demand to be loved as intensely as they love. If they are equally lovable, such a claim may be justified, but when they have nothing lovable about them, it is absurd. Perhaps, however, contrary does not truly aim at contrary by its own nature, but only incidentally -- the real desire is for what is intermediate, for that is what is good. It is good for the dry not to become wet, but to reach the mean, and similarly with the hot and all other extremes.

But let us set aside these questions, as they are somewhat removed from our inquiry.
elegant:
Unequals become friends through proportional love, which creates equality. Likeness in virtue makes the strongest bond: the virtuous are steadfast in themselves and in each other, neither committing nor tolerating wrongdoing. The wicked lack even self-consistency and bond only briefly through shared vice.

Utility friendship naturally arises between opposites -- rich and poor, learned and ignorant -- each supplying what the other lacks. Lovers demanding equal love when they themselves are unlovable are ridiculous. But perhaps opposites do not truly seek each other by nature; rather, each seeks the intermediate state, which is the good. The dry does not seek the wet but the balanced middle. These physical questions, however, lie outside our inquiry.
summary: Unequal friends achieve equality through proportional love. The virtuous form the steadiest bonds because they are consistent in themselves. The wicked are inconstant even with themselves. Utility friendships naturally arise between opposites who complement each other's deficiencies.
distilled: Unequals can achieve friendship through proportional love, but lasting steadfastness belongs only to those who are alike in virtue and consistent within themselves.
notes: The claim that the wicked 'do not remain even like to themselves' is developed at length in IX.4, one of the most psychologically penetrating passages in the Ethics, where Aristotle argues the vicious person is in inner conflict. -- The idea that opposites attract incidentally, with the true aim being the intermediate, connects to Aristotle's doctrine of the mean in Book II. -- The physical theory about contraries seeking the mean reflects Aristotle's natural philosophy in Physics and On Generation and Corruption. -- This passage concludes the initial survey of friendship types before Aristotle turns to the relationship between friendship and justice.

refashioned:
Friendship and justice seem, as we said at the outset, to be concerned with the same things and to exist between the same people. In every community there appears to be some form of justice and also some form of friendship. People address as friends their fellow-voyagers, fellow-soldiers, and all those associated with them in any kind of community. The extent of their association is the extent of their friendship, and equally the extent to which justice exists between them. The proverb 'what friends have is common property' expresses the truth, for friendship depends on community.

Brothers and close companions share all things in common, while others share only certain things -- some more, some fewer. Accordingly, some friendships are more genuine than others, and the claims of justice vary as well. The duties of parents to children differ from those of brothers to each other, and those of comrades differ from those of fellow citizens -- and likewise for every other kind of friendship.

The acts that count as unjust also differ according to the relationship, and the injustice grows worse in proportion to the closeness of the friendship. It is more terrible to defraud a companion than a fellow citizen, more terrible to refuse help to a brother than to a stranger, and more terrible to wound a father than anyone else. The demands of justice increase in step with the depth of the friendship -- which shows that friendship and justice exist between the same people and extend equally far.
elegant:
Friendship and justice concern the same objects and exist between the same people. Every community involves both: fellow-voyagers, fellow-soldiers, and all associates call each other friends, and their friendship extends as far as their shared life -- as does the justice between them. 'What friends have is common property,' for friendship rests on community.

Different relationships involve different degrees of sharing and thus different degrees of friendship and different duties of justice. Injustice is graver in proportion to the closeness of the bond: defrauding a companion is worse than defrauding a citizen, neglecting a brother worse than neglecting a stranger, wounding a father worse than wounding anyone else. Friendship and justice grow together.
summary: Friendship and justice are found in the same relationships and grow together in proportion. Every community involves both. The closer the friendship, the greater the obligations of justice -- and the more terrible the injustice when those obligations are violated.
distilled: Friendship and justice are coextensive: the deeper the bond of friendship, the greater the demands of justice and the graver the wrong when they are violated.
notes: The parallel treatment of friendship and justice is a distinctive feature of Aristotle's Ethics, connecting Book VIII to Book V's treatment of justice. -- The proverb 'what friends have is common property' (koina ta philōn) was attributed to Pythagoras and was widely cited in Greek philosophy; Plato discusses it in Republic V (449c). -- The principle that injustice increases with the closeness of the relationship has significant implications for family ethics and remains influential in moral philosophy. -- This chapter begins the systematic correlation of forms of friendship with forms of political community that continues through VIII.10-11.

refashioned:
All forms of community are like parts of the political community. People come together for some particular advantage and to provide something they need for life. The political community itself seems to have originated and to persist for the sake of advantage, since this is what legislators aim at, calling just whatever serves the common good.

The other communities pursue advantage in specific, limited ways. Sailors seek what is advantageous on a voyage -- whether money or something similar. Fellow soldiers seek what is advantageous in war -- whether wealth, victory, or the capture of a city. Members of tribes and local districts act similarly. Some communities seem to exist for the sake of pleasure -- religious guilds for the sake of sacrifice, and social clubs for companionship. But all of these fall under the political community, which aims not at immediate advantage but at what is advantageous for life as a whole. It organizes sacrifices and gatherings for this purpose, assigns honors to the gods, and provides pleasant recreation for the citizens. The ancient sacrificial festivals and gatherings seem to have taken place after the harvest as a kind of offering of firstfruits, because these were the seasons when people had the most leisure.

All communities, then, are parts of the political community, and the particular kinds of friendship correspond to the particular kinds of community.
elegant: All communities are parts of the political community. People associate for particular advantages -- sailors for profitable voyages, soldiers for victory, tribesmen for local goods. Some communities aim at pleasure: religious guilds at sacrifice, social clubs at companionship. But all fall under the political community, which pursues not immediate benefit but the good of life as a whole, organizing worship, festivals, and civic life accordingly. Ancient harvest festivals exemplify this -- celebrations held when leisure permitted. Each kind of community generates its corresponding kind of friendship.
summary: Every form of human community -- from sailing crews to religious guilds -- is a part of the larger political community, which aims at the good of life as a whole rather than immediate advantage. Each type of community produces its own corresponding form of friendship.
distilled: All human communities are parts of the political community, and each form of association generates its own corresponding kind of friendship.
notes: The claim that the polis exists for the sake of the good life (not merely life) echoes Politics I.2 (1252b29-30) and III.9. -- Religious guilds (thiasoi) and social clubs (eranoi) were common institutions in Greek civic life; eranoi involved collective meals and mutual financial support. -- The observation about harvest festivals connects to Aristotle's interest in the historical development of institutions. -- 'What legislators call just is whatever serves the common advantage' reflects the discussion of legal justice in Book V.1. -- This passage prepares for the detailed mapping of friendship onto constitutional forms in VIII.10-11.

refashioned:
There are three kinds of political constitution, and an equal number of deviations -- perversions, as it were. The constitutions are monarchy, aristocracy, and a third based on property qualification, which it seems fitting to call timocracy, though most people simply call it polity. The best of these is monarchy; the worst is timocracy.

The deviation from monarchy is tyranny. Both are forms of one-person rule, but there is the greatest difference between them: the tyrant looks to his own advantage, the king to that of his subjects. For no one is truly a king unless he is self-sufficient and excels his subjects in every good thing; such a person needs nothing more and therefore will not pursue his own interests but those of his subjects. A king who fails this standard is merely a king in name. Tyranny is the very opposite of this -- the tyrant pursues his own good. That tyranny is the worst deviation is clearer precisely because it is the contrary of the best.

Monarchy degenerates into tyranny, since tyranny is the corrupt form of one-person rule and a bad king becomes a tyrant. Aristocracy degenerates into oligarchy through the corruption of the rulers, who distribute the city's goods contrary to merit -- giving all or most good things to themselves, assigning offices always to the same people, and valuing wealth above all else. The result is rule by a few bad men rather than by the most worthy. Timocracy degenerates into democracy, since the two border on each other: timocracy, too, aspires to majority rule, and all who meet the property qualification count as equal. Democracy is the least harmful of the deviations, being only a slight departure from its proper form. These, then, are the transformations to which constitutions are most liable, since they represent the smallest and easiest transitions.
elegant: Three constitutions exist -- monarchy, aristocracy, and timocracy -- with three corresponding deviations: tyranny, oligarchy, and democracy. Monarchy is best, serving subjects' interests; tyranny is worst, serving only the ruler's. The king is self-sufficient and excels in all goods; the tyrant pursues only his own. Aristocracy corrupts into oligarchy when rulers distribute goods by wealth rather than merit. Timocracy shades into democracy, the least harmful deviation, since both aim at majority rule and equality among qualified citizens. Each constitution degenerates into its neighboring deviation through the smallest possible transition.
summary: The three legitimate constitutions -- monarchy, aristocracy, and timocracy -- each have a corresponding deviation: tyranny, oligarchy, and democracy. Monarchy is best because the king serves his subjects; tyranny is worst because the tyrant serves only himself. Each constitution tends to degenerate into its neighboring corrupt form.
distilled: The three constitutions degenerate into their corresponding deviations -- monarchy into tyranny, aristocracy into oligarchy, timocracy into democracy -- through the corruption of rulers who pursue private interest over common good.
notes: This constitutional taxonomy is treated at greater length in Politics III.7 and IV.2. -- Timocracy (timokratia, from timē, 'honor/property assessment') is Aristotle's term for a constitution based on property qualification; the more common term 'polity' (politeia) is what most people use, as Aristotle notes. -- The idea that constitutions degenerate in a cycle is shared with Plato's Republic VIII, though Aristotle's scheme differs in details. -- The claim that democracy is the 'least bad' deviation differs from Plato's ranking in the Statesman (302e-303b), where democracy is worst among lawful constitutions but best among lawless ones.

refashioned:
One can find resemblances to these constitutions -- patterns of them, as it were -- even within households. The association of a father with his sons has the form of monarchy, since the father cares for his children. This is why Homer calls Zeus 'father' -- monarchy ideally is paternal rule. But among the Persians, the father's rule is tyrannical, for they treat their sons as slaves. The rule of a master over slaves is also tyrannical, since it operates for the master's advantage. This form seems correct in the case of actual slavery, but the Persian variety is perverted, since different relationships call for different modes of authority.

The association of husband and wife resembles aristocracy: the man rules in matters suited to his worth and hands over to his wife those matters that befit her. If the man rules in everything, the relationship becomes an oligarchy, since he is not acting according to their respective worth or ruling by virtue of genuine superiority. Sometimes, however, women rule because they are heiresses -- their authority rests not on excellence but on wealth and power, just as in oligarchies.

The association of brothers resembles timocracy, for they are equals except insofar as they differ in age. If the age difference is great, the friendship is no longer of the brotherly type.

Democracy is found chiefly in households without a master -- where everyone is on equal footing -- and in those where the nominal ruler is weak and everyone has license to do as he pleases.
elegant: Household relationships mirror political constitutions. Father-son resembles monarchy -- paternal care for subjects, which is why Homer calls Zeus 'father.' But Persian fathers rule tyrannically, treating sons as slaves. Husband-wife resembles aristocracy: each rules in their proper sphere according to worth. When the husband dominates everything, it becomes oligarchy. When wives rule as heiresses, authority rests on wealth, not merit -- also oligarchic. Brotherhood resembles timocracy: equals differing only in age. Democracy appears in masterless households where all are equal, or where the ruler is weak and everyone does as they please.
summary: Household relationships mirror political constitutions: father-son resembles monarchy, husband-wife resembles aristocracy, brotherhood resembles timocracy, and masterless households resemble democracy. Each relationship can also degenerate into its corresponding corrupt form.
distilled: Every political constitution has its household counterpart -- the father is monarch, husband and wife form an aristocracy, brothers a timocracy, and masterless households a democracy.
notes: Homer calls Zeus 'father of men and gods' in multiple passages (e.g., Iliad I.544). -- The reference to Persian fathers treating sons as slaves reflects Greek perceptions of Persian despotism, a common theme in Greek political thought (cf. Herodotus, Histories). -- The comparison of household to polis is central to Politics I, where Aristotle argues the household is prior to the state but the state is prior in nature. -- The observation about heiress-wives (epiklēroi) reflects Athenian law, where an heiress could inherit her father's estate and the nearest male relative had the right to marry her. -- Aristotle's treatment of husband-wife relations as aristocratic rather than monarchical represents a more egalitarian view than some ancient accounts, though still hierarchical by modern standards.

refashioned:
Each type of constitution can be seen to involve friendship to exactly the degree that it involves justice. The friendship between a king and his subjects depends on an abundance of benefits bestowed. The king confers benefits on his subjects because, being a good man, he cares for their wellbeing -- as a shepherd cares for his sheep. This is why Homer called Agamemnon 'shepherd of the peoples.'

The friendship of a father is similar, though it surpasses even the king's in the magnitude of its benefits. The father is responsible for his children's very existence -- which is considered the greatest good -- as well as for their nurture and upbringing.
elegant: Each constitution involves friendship in proportion to its justice. The king's friendship with his subjects rests on the abundance of benefits he provides, caring for them as a shepherd tends his flock -- hence Homer's epithet for Agamemnon, 'shepherd of the peoples.' A father's friendship resembles a king's but exceeds it: the father gives his children existence itself -- the greatest of goods -- along with nurture and upbringing.
summary: The friendship between a king and his subjects mirrors that of a father and his children, both resting on the superior party's beneficence. A father's bond surpasses even a king's because he provides the greatest good of all -- existence itself.
distilled: A king's friendship with his subjects, like a father's with his children, rests on conferring benefits -- but the father's surpasses all others by giving the gift of existence.
notes: Homer calls Agamemnon 'shepherd of the peoples' (poimēn laōn) repeatedly in the Iliad (e.g., II.243). The metaphor of ruler as shepherd was common in ancient Near Eastern and Greek political thought. -- The idea that existence is the greatest good echoes discussions in Book I about what constitutes the highest good. -- The parallel between kingly and paternal authority is developed further in the next section and has significant implications for Aristotle's political philosophy, particularly his discussion of kingship in Politics III.14-17.

refashioned:
These same qualities are ascribed to ancestors as well. Furthermore, it is natural for a father to rule over his sons, ancestors over descendants, and a king over his subjects. These friendships involve the superiority of one party over the other, which is why ancestors are honored. The justice that exists between such persons is therefore not the same on both sides but is in every case proportioned to merit -- and the same is true of the friendship.

The friendship of husband and wife is the same as that found in an aristocracy: the better partner receives more of what is good, and each receives what befits them. The justice in these relations follows the same pattern.

The friendship of brothers is like that of comrades: they are equal and of similar age, and such people tend to be alike in their feelings and character. This also resembles the friendship appropriate to timocratic government, where the ideal is for citizens to be equal and fair, and therefore they rule in turn and on equal terms. The friendship appropriate to such a constitution corresponds accordingly.
elegant: Ancestors too are honored for their superiority, as fathers naturally rule sons and kings rule subjects. Justice and friendship in these unequal relationships are proportioned to merit. Husband-wife friendship mirrors aristocracy: the worthier partner receives more, and each receives what befits them. Brotherhood resembles comradeship and timocracy: equals of similar age and character who rule in turn on equal terms.
summary: In unequal relationships like parent-child or ruler-subject, both friendship and justice are proportioned to each party's merit. Husband-wife friendship mirrors aristocracy, while brotherhood mirrors the equal, turn-taking friendship of timocratic citizenship.
distilled: Each form of friendship mirrors its political counterpart: unequal bonds demand proportional justice, while brotherly friendship mirrors the equal, reciprocal relations of timocratic citizens.
notes: The principle that justice is 'not the same on both sides' in unequal relationships connects directly to the discussion of proportional justice in Book V.3-4. -- The analogy between comradeship (hetairia) and brotherhood reflects the importance of age-group associations in Greek social life. -- The timocratic ideal of ruling 'in turn' (en merei) is central to Aristotle's concept of political citizenship in Politics III.1, where he defines the citizen as one who shares in ruling and being ruled.

refashioned:
In the deviant constitutions, friendship barely exists, just as justice barely exists. It is least present in the worst form. In tyranny there is little or no friendship, for where ruler and ruled have nothing in common, there can be no friendship -- just as there can be no justice. The relationship is like that between a craftsman and his tool, or between soul and body, or between master and slave: in each case the latter is benefited by the one who uses it, but there is no friendship or justice toward lifeless things.

There is no friendship toward a horse or an ox either, nor toward a slave insofar as he is a slave. For master and slave have nothing in common: the slave is a living tool and the tool a lifeless slave. Insofar as someone is a slave, then, one cannot be friends with him. But insofar as he is a human being, one can. For there seems to be some form of justice between any person and any other who can participate in a system of law or enter into agreements. Therefore there can also be friendship with a slave insofar as he is a human being.

Thus, while friendship and justice barely exist in tyrannies, they exist more fully in democracies, where citizens are equal and have much in common.
elegant: In deviant constitutions, friendship exists barely, mirroring the scarcity of justice.
