Tyranny has the least friendship: where ruler and ruled share nothing, neither friendship nor justice is possible -- as between craftsman and tool, soul and body, master and slave. There is no friendship with lifeless things, animals, or slaves qua slaves, since there is nothing in common. But with a slave qua human being, friendship is possible, since justice exists between any persons who can share in law and agreements. Friendship and justice exist most fully in democracies, where citizens are equal and share much in common.
summary: Friendship requires something in common between the parties. In tyranny, where ruler and ruled share nothing, friendship barely exists. A slave cannot be befriended as a slave but can as a human being. Democracy, where citizens are equal, produces the most friendship and justice.
distilled: Friendship requires common ground between the parties -- tyranny destroys it by eliminating what ruler and ruled share, while democracy fosters it through civic equality.
notes: The description of the slave as a 'living tool' (empsuchon organon) echoes Politics I.4 (1253b33). Aristotle's view of slavery is one of the most criticized aspects of his philosophy. -- The concession that a slave can be befriended 'qua human being' creates a tension with Aristotle's defense of natural slavery in Politics I.4-7. -- The soul-body analogy reflects Aristotle's hylomorphic metaphysics (De Anima II.1). -- The claim that democracy has the most friendship because of equality among citizens contrasts interestingly with Aristotle's generally more favorable view of aristocracy and mixed constitutions in the Politics. -- This passage has been important in debates about Aristotle's views on human dignity and universal moral status.

refashioned:
Every form of friendship, then, involves association, as we have said. One might, however, distinguish from the rest both the friendship of kindred and that of comrades. The friendships of fellow-citizens, fellow-tribesmen, and fellow-voyagers are more like friendships of association -- they seem to rest on a kind of agreement. The friendship between host and guest may be placed in this group.

The friendship of kinsmen itself, while it appears to take many forms, seems in every case to be rooted in parental friendship. Parents love their children as a part of themselves, and children love their parents as the source from which they originated.
elegant: All friendship involves association, but kinship and comradeship stand apart from the rest. Civic, tribal, and travel friendships are mere friendships of association, resting on agreement -- as does the bond between host and guest. The many forms of kinship friendship all trace back to the parental bond: parents love children as parts of themselves, and children love parents as their origin.
summary: All friendships involve some form of association, but kinship and comradeship are distinctive. Civic and professional friendships rest on agreements. All forms of kinship friendship ultimately derive from the foundational bond between parent and child.
distilled: All kinship friendships trace back to the parental bond, in which parents love children as extensions of themselves and children love parents as their origin.
notes: The distinction between friendship based on kinship (suggeneia) and friendship based on compact or agreement (sunthēkē) maps onto the distinction between natural and conventional justice in Book V.7. -- The friendship of host and guest (xenia) was one of the most sacred institutions in Greek life, protected by Zeus Xenios. -- The idea that parents love children 'as a part of themselves' is developed more fully in the next section and anticipates IX.7, where Aristotle argues that the producer's love for the product exceeds the reverse.

refashioned:
There are three reasons why parents' love exceeds that of children. First, parents know their offspring better than children know that these adults are their parents. Second, the originator feels his offspring to be his own more than the offspring feel their begetter to be theirs -- the product belongs to the producer (as a tooth or a strand of hair belongs to the person whose it is), but the producer does not belong to the product, or belongs in a lesser degree. Third, time produces the same effect: parents love their children from the moment of birth, while children come to love their parents only after time has elapsed and they have developed understanding or at least the capacity of sensory recognition.

From these considerations it is also clear why mothers love more than fathers. Parents love their children as other selves, since their offspring are, by virtue of their separate existence, a kind of extension of themselves. Children love their parents as the beings from whom they were born. Brothers love each other as born from the same parents, for their shared identity with those parents makes them identical with each other -- hence people speak of 'the same blood,' 'the same stock,' and similar expressions. Brothers are, in a sense, the same thing existing in separate individuals.

Two things contribute greatly to friendship: a common upbringing and similarity of age. As the saying goes, 'two of an age take to each other,' and people raised together tend to become comrades. This is why the friendship of brothers resembles that of comrades. Cousins and other kinsmen are connected through their derivation from common ancestors -- from brothers and therefore from the same parents. They become closer or more distant according to the nearness or distance of the original ancestor.
elegant:
Parents love children more than children love parents for three reasons: parents know their children better; the originator feels the product is his own more than the reverse; and time gives parents a head start in love. Mothers love most because of these factors. Parents love children as other selves -- extensions of their own being in separate existence. Children love parents as their source. Brothers share identity through common parents, hence 'same blood, same stock' -- they are the same thing in separate persons.

Common upbringing and similar age greatly foster friendship: 'two of an age take to each other.' Brotherhood thus resembles comradeship. Cousins and wider kin connect through shared ancestors, growing closer or more distant according to genealogical proximity.
summary: Parents love children more than children love parents because they know them better, feel stronger ownership, and begin loving earlier. Parents see children as extensions of themselves, while brothers share identity through common origin. Common upbringing and similar age are powerful bonds, making brotherhood akin to comradeship.
distilled: Parents love children more intensely because the originator's bond to the product is stronger than the reverse -- parents see children as other selves existing separately.
notes: The argument that the producer loves the product more than vice versa is developed further in IX.7, where Aristotle compares it to the poet's love for his poems. -- The claim that mothers love more than fathers has been much discussed; Aristotle seems to ground it partly in greater certainty of parentage and partly in the mother's more direct role in bringing the child into being. -- 'Two of an age take to each other' is proverbial in Greek; cf. Homer, Odyssey XVII.218: 'god always brings like to like.' -- The analysis of kinship as degrees of shared identity through common ancestors anticipates modern discussions of kin selection in evolutionary biology, though Aristotle's framework is philosophical rather than biological.

refashioned:
The friendship of children toward parents, and of human beings toward gods, is a relationship with something good and superior. Parents and gods have conferred the greatest benefits: they are the causes of our existence, our nourishment, and our education from birth. This kind of friendship also possesses both pleasantness and utility to a greater degree than friendship with strangers, since the lives of parents and children are more deeply intertwined.

The friendship of brothers has the same characteristics found in that of comrades -- and especially when the comrades are good -- and in general between people who are alike. Brothers belong to each other more closely and begin with a love for each other from their very birth. Those born of the same parents, raised together, and similarly educated are more alike in character. And the test of time has been applied most fully and convincingly in their case.
elegant:
Children's friendship toward parents, like humans' toward gods, is directed at something good and superior. Parents and gods give the greatest benefits: existence, nourishment, and education. This bond surpasses stranger-friendship in both pleasure and utility because of the deeper intertwining of their lives.

Brothers share the virtues of comradeship -- especially when both are good -- with the added advantages of belonging to each other from birth, being alike in upbringing and character, and having their bond tested by the longest familiarity.
summary: The bond between children and parents, like that between humans and gods, rests on gratitude for the greatest benefits -- existence, nourishment, and education. Brotherhood combines the qualities of comradeship with deeper natural bonds and longer-tested familiarity.
distilled: The love of children for parents and of humans for gods is directed at benefactors of the highest order -- those responsible for our very existence and formation.
notes: The parallel between parental and divine friendship is significant for Aristotle's theology; in Metaphysics XII, God is the unmoved mover who inspires love but may not reciprocate it, creating a philosophical puzzle about whether friendship with God is possible (see also 8.19). -- The emphasis on common upbringing (suntrophia) as a source of friendship reflects the importance Aristotle places on habituation in moral development (Book II). -- The 'test of time' criterion connects to the proverb about 'eating salt together' in 8.7.

refashioned:
Among other kinsmen, friendly relations are found in appropriate proportion. Between husband and wife, friendship seems to exist by nature. Human beings are naturally inclined to form couples -- even more than to form political communities, since the household is prior to and more necessary than the city, and reproduction is something humans share with all animals.

With other animals, the union extends only to reproduction. But human beings live together not only for the sake of having children but also for the various purposes of life. From the very start, their functions are divided: the tasks of husband and wife are different, and so they help each other by contributing their distinct abilities to the common stock. For these reasons, both utility and pleasure seem to be found in this kind of friendship. But this friendship may also be grounded in virtue, if both parties are good -- for each has a distinctive excellence, and they can take delight in that.

Children seem to serve as a bond of union, which is why childless couples part more easily. Children are a good shared by both, and what is held in common holds people together.

How husband and wife -- and friends in general -- ought to behave toward each other appears to be the same question as how it is just for them to behave. For a person clearly does not have the same duties toward a friend, a stranger, a comrade, and a schoolfellow.
elegant: Kinsmen are friends in due proportion. Husband and wife are friends by nature: humans are inclined to pair even more than to form cities, since the household is prior to and more necessary than the state. Unlike other animals, humans live together not merely for reproduction but for all of life's purposes, dividing functions and pooling different abilities. This yields both utility and pleasure -- and virtue too, if both are good, since each has a distinctive excellence. Children bind couples together as a shared good; childless pairs separate more easily. How spouses and friends should treat each other is the same question as what justice requires between them, and these duties differ with each type of relationship.
summary: Marriage is a natural friendship grounded in the complementary contributions of husband and wife, yielding utility, pleasure, and potentially virtue. Children strengthen the bond as a shared good. How friends should behave toward each other is fundamentally a question of justice, which varies by relationship.
distilled: Marriage is the most natural human partnership, prior even to the city, yielding utility, pleasure, and virtue through the complementary contributions of husband and wife.
notes: The claim that the household is 'earlier and more necessary' than the city echoes Politics I.2, though there Aristotle also argues the city is prior 'in nature' (by final cause). -- The idea that human coupling surpasses animal mating in its purposes reflects Aristotle's characteristic emphasis on humans' rational and social nature. -- The observation about childless couples parting more easily is an empirical claim about Greek social life, where marriage was closely tied to the production of legitimate heirs. -- The final remark about different duties to different relationships sets up the discussion of complaints and justice in friendship in VIII.13-14.

refashioned:
There are three kinds of friendship, as we said at the beginning of our inquiry, and within each kind some friends are equals while others stand in a relationship of superiority. Not only can equally good people become friends, but a better person can also befriend a worse one. The same holds for friendships of pleasure or utility -- the friends may be equal or unequal in the benefits they provide.

This being so, equals must achieve the required balance on the basis of equality in love and in all other respects, while unequals must render what is proportional to their superiority or inferiority.

Complaints and reproaches arise either only or chiefly in friendships of utility, and this is to be expected. Those who are friends on the basis of virtue are eager to do well by each other, since this is a mark of both virtue and friendship. Between people who vie with each other in doing good, there can be no complaints or quarrels: no one is offended by someone who loves him and treats him well. If a person has any refinement of feeling, he takes his revenge by doing even more good in return. And the one who surpasses the other in the services he renders will not complain, since he gets what he aims at -- for each person desires what is good.

Nor do complaints arise much in friendships of pleasure. Both parties get what they want at the same time, if they enjoy spending time together. Anyone who complained that the other person was not giving him pleasure would seem ridiculous, since he is free to stop spending time with that person.
elegant: Within each of the three kinds of friendship, friends may be equals or unequals. Equals must balance love equally; unequals must give proportionally to their standing. Complaints arise chiefly in utility friendships. Virtue friends eagerly do good for each other and cannot quarrel -- the refined person 'retaliates' by doing more good. The one who gives more is not aggrieved, since giving good is precisely what he wants. Pleasure friends rarely complain either, since both enjoy the shared time, and anyone dissatisfied is free to leave.
summary: Within all three kinds of friendship, equals must balance love equally while unequals must give proportionally. Complaints arise mainly in utility friendships, since virtue friends compete in doing good and pleasure friends are satisfied by shared enjoyment.
distilled: Complaints in friendship arise almost exclusively from utility, since virtue friends compete in generosity and pleasure friends are satisfied by shared enjoyment.
notes: The idea that virtuous friends 'compete' in doing good (hamillōntai) presents an attractive picture of moral friendship as mutually elevating. -- The claim that the superior giver in a virtue friendship is not aggrieved because 'each person desires what is good' connects to the discussion of self-love in IX.8. -- This passage begins the treatment of complaints and dissolution in friendship, which continues through VIII.13-14 and is further developed in IX.1-3.

refashioned: The friendship of utility, however, is full of complaints. Since each party uses the other for his own benefit, each always wants to get the better of the bargain. Each thinks he is getting less than he deserves and blames his partner for not providing everything he wants and is owed. Meanwhile, those who do good for others cannot help their beneficiaries as much as those beneficiaries would like.
elegant: Utility friendship breeds constant complaint. Each party seeks the better bargain, feels shortchanged, and blames the other for not providing enough. Benefactors can never fully satisfy those they help.
summary: Utility friendships are plagued by complaints because each side wants to gain more than they give and feels they deserve more than they receive.
distilled: Utility friendships breed constant grievance because each party measures the relationship by what they receive and inevitably feels shortchanged.
notes: This brief but incisive observation captures the inherent instability of instrumental relationships. -- The dynamic described here connects to the discussion of exchange justice in Book V.5, where Aristotle analyzes how economic transactions require proportional reciprocity. -- The complaint dynamic anticipates modern economic and psychological analyses of transactional relationships.

refashioned:
It seems that, just as justice is of two kinds -- one unwritten and the other legal -- so too there are two kinds of utility friendship: one moral and the other legal. Complaints arise most of all when people do not dissolve the relationship in the same spirit as the one in which they formed it.

The legal type operates on fixed terms. Its purely commercial variety requires immediate payment, while the more generous variety allows time but stipulates a definite exchange. In the latter, the debt is clear and unambiguous, though the deferral contains an element of friendliness. This is why some states do not permit lawsuits arising from such agreements, holding that those who bargain on credit must accept the consequences.

The moral type operates without fixed terms. A person gives -- or does whatever he does -- as though giving to a friend, but he expects to receive as much or more in return, as though he had lent rather than given. If he finds himself worse off when the relationship ends than when it began, he will complain. This happens because everyone, while wishing for what is noble, chooses what is advantageous. It is noble to benefit another without thought of repayment, but it is advantageous to receive benefits.

Therefore, if we can, we should return the equivalent of what we have received. We must not make someone our friend against his will; rather, we should recognize that we were mistaken at the outset and accepted a benefit from someone we should not have -- since it was not from a true friend, nor from someone acting purely for the sake of giving -- and we must settle up as if we had agreed to fixed terms. Indeed, one would agree to repay if one could -- and if one could not, even the giver would not have expected repayment.

So if it is possible, we must repay. But at the outset we should consider who is benefiting us and on what terms, so that we may either accept the benefit on those terms or decline it.
elegant:
Just as justice divides into written and unwritten, utility friendship divides into legal and moral. Complaints arise when parties dissolve relationships in a different spirit from how they formed them. Legal utility friendship has fixed terms -- either immediate exchange or deferred payment with a clear debt. Moral utility friendship has no fixed terms: one gives as to a friend but expects equal or greater return, treating gifts as loans. When the giver ends up worse off, he complains.

This happens because people wish for the noble but choose the advantageous. Giving without expecting return is noble; receiving benefits is advantageous. We should repay what we receive -- recognizing our error if we accepted from the wrong person -- and settle as if terms had been fixed. At the outset, we should carefully consider who benefits us and on what terms, so we can accept or decline accordingly.
summary: Utility friendship has two forms: legal (with fixed terms of exchange) and moral (without fixed terms, where gifts are implicitly treated as loans). Complaints arise when parties disagree about which type they were in. We should repay benefits when possible, and should carefully consider the terms on which we accept them.
distilled: Most disputes in friendship arise from confusion between gift and loan -- when one party gives as to a friend but expects repayment as from a debtor.
notes: The distinction between written and unwritten justice echoes Book V.7's discussion of natural versus conventional justice. -- The analysis of moral versus legal utility friendship is one of the most practically oriented passages in the Ethics, dealing with the everyday problems of obligation and expectation. -- Some Greek states, including Athens, had variable policies about enforcing informal credit agreements, as Aristotle notes. -- The psychological observation that people 'wish for what is noble but choose what is advantageous' is a recurring theme in Aristotle's moral psychology, related to the discussion of weakness of will (akrasia) in Book VII. -- This analysis is continued and refined in IX.1-2.

refashioned:
It is a matter of dispute whether we should measure a service by its value to the receiver and calculate repayment accordingly, or by the generosity of the giver. Those who have received benefits tend to say that what they received meant little to the giver and could have been obtained from others -- thus minimizing the service. The givers, on the contrary, claim it was the greatest thing they had, that it could not have been obtained from anyone else, and that it was given in times of danger or similar need.

If the friendship is one that aims at utility, then surely the benefit to the receiver is the proper measure. The receiver is the one who asked for the service, and the giver helps on the assumption that he will receive an equivalent return. The assistance has been exactly as great as the advantage to the receiver, and so he should return as much as he received -- or even more, since that would be nobler.

In friendships based on virtue, by contrast, complaints do not arise, and the purpose of the giver serves as a kind of measure. For it is in purpose that the essential element of virtue and character resides.
elegant: Should a service be measured by its value to the receiver or the sacrifice of the giver? Receivers minimize: 'it cost you little and I could have gotten it elsewhere.' Givers maximize: 'it was my greatest possession, given in your hour of need.' In utility friendship, the receiver's benefit is the proper measure, since the giver helped expecting equivalent return -- so the receiver should repay accordingly, or more if he can. In virtue friendship, complaints do not arise: the giver's intention is the measure, since purpose is the essence of virtue and character.
summary: In utility friendships, services should be measured by their value to the receiver, since the giver expects equivalent return. In virtue friendships, the giver's intention is what matters, since character is defined by purpose rather than outcomes.
distilled: In utility friendships the measure of a service is its value to the receiver; in virtue friendships the measure is the intention of the giver.
notes: The debate about measuring services from giver's versus receiver's perspective remains live in moral philosophy and in discussions of gift economies versus market economies. -- The claim that 'purpose' (prohairesis) is the essential element of virtue and character is one of Aristotle's most important ethical claims, emphasized in Book II.4 and III.2-3. -- This passage is closely connected to the fuller treatment of repayment obligations in IX.2, where Aristotle discusses cases like repaying a benefactor versus helping a friend. -- The rhetorical strategies of minimizers and maximizers that Aristotle describes reflect the Greek forensic tradition familiar from the law courts.

refashioned:
Disagreements also arise in friendships based on superiority. Each party expects to receive more from the friendship, and when this expectation is not met, the friendship dissolves. The superior in virtue thinks he should receive more, since more should be assigned to a good person. The more useful party similarly thinks he deserves more -- arguing that a useless person should not receive an equal share, since the relationship becomes a public service rather than a friendship if its returns do not match the worth of the contributions.

They reason that friendship should work like a business partnership: those who invest more should earn more. But the person in need or in an inferior position makes the opposite claim: it is the very purpose of friendship with a good or powerful person to provide help. What use is it, they ask, to be the friend of someone good or powerful if you get nothing from it?
elegant: In friendships of superiority, each side claims more. The virtuous person argues he deserves more because goodness merits greater reward. The useful person argues the same -- otherwise the relationship is charity, not friendship. Both reason like business partners: greater contribution should yield greater return. But the needy and inferior counter: the whole point of befriending someone good or powerful is to receive help. What use is such a friend if you gain nothing?
summary: Friendships between unequals breed disagreements because each side expects to receive more. The superior claims greater return for greater worth or contribution; the inferior claims that helping the needy is the very purpose of friendship with the powerful.
distilled: Friendships between unequals breed conflict because the superior expects reward proportional to worth while the inferior expects help proportional to need.
notes: The business partnership analogy (koinōnia) connects to the broader discussion of commercial justice in Book V.5. -- The tension between merit-based and need-based claims on friendship resources foreshadows modern debates in distributive justice between desert-based and needs-based principles. -- This passage sets up the resolution in VIII.14 (8.38), where Aristotle argues that each party should receive more of different things. -- The problem of unequal friendships was already raised in VIII.7 (8.18-8.19) and receives its fullest treatment here.

refashioned: At any rate, it seems that each party is justified in his claim, and that each should indeed receive more from the friendship than the other -- but not more of the same thing. The superior party should receive more honor, while the inferior should receive more material gain. Honor is the fitting reward for virtue and beneficence, while gain is the assistance that inferiority requires.
elegant: Both sides are justified: each should receive more -- but of different things. The superior should receive more honor, the fitting reward for virtue and beneficence. The inferior should receive more material gain, the assistance their position requires.
summary: The solution to disputes in unequal friendships is that each party should receive more, but of different things: the superior receives more honor and the inferior receives more material benefit.
distilled: In unequal friendships, the superior rightly receives more honor while the inferior rightly receives more material help -- each getting more of what they need.
notes: This elegant resolution parallels the structure of distributive justice in Book V.3, where Aristotle argues that equality is achieved through proportion rather than identical shares. -- The distinction between honor (timē) and gain (kerdos) as different currencies of social exchange is fundamental to Greek ethical thinking and anticipates modern discussions of multiple forms of social capital. -- This solution applies to all the unequal relationships discussed in VIII.7 and VIII.10-11: parent-child, ruler-subject, husband-wife.

refashioned:
The same principle seems to hold in constitutional arrangements. The person who contributes nothing good to the common stock receives no honor, for what belongs to the public is given to whoever benefits the public, and honor is a public good. One cannot simultaneously extract wealth from the common fund and also receive public honor. No one tolerates getting the lesser share in everything, so the one who loses in wealth is compensated with honor, and the one who is willing to accept payment receives wealth instead. Distribution according to merit equalizes the parties and preserves friendship, as we have said.

This is also how we should associate with unequals. The person who is benefited in terms of wealth or virtue must give honor in return, repaying what he can. Friendship asks a person to do what he can, not what is strictly proportional to what he has received -- since strict proportionality is not always possible. Consider the honors paid to the gods or to parents: no one could ever return to them the equivalent of what he has received, but the person who serves them to the utmost of his ability is considered a good person.

This is why it would seem wrong for a son to disown his father, though a father may disown his son. The son, being in debt, should repay. But nothing a son can do would be equivalent to what he has received, so he remains always in debt. Creditors, however, can forgive a debt, and a father can therefore do so as well.

At the same time, it is generally assumed that no one would disown a son unless that son had sunk deeply into wickedness. Apart from the natural friendship between father and son, it is simply human nature not to reject a son's help. But the son, if he is wicked, will naturally avoid helping his father, or will not do so eagerly -- for most people wish to receive benefits but avoid providing them, seeing generosity as unprofitable.

Let this suffice for our discussion of these questions.
elegant:
The same principle governs constitutional arrangements. Public honor goes to those who benefit the community; no one gets both maximum wealth and maximum honor from the common stock. Distribution according to merit equalizes and preserves friendship.

With unequals, the beneficiary should return honor for what he can. Friendship demands what is possible, not strict equivalence -- for some debts can never be fully repaid. No one can return to gods or parents the equivalent of existence, nourishment, and education, but serving them to the utmost of one's ability suffices.

A son should not disown his father, being permanently in debt; but a father may disown a son, as creditors may forgive debts. Yet normally no one would abandon a son unless he were deeply wicked -- natural affection and self-interest both resist it. A wicked son, however, may avoid helping his father, since most people prefer receiving benefits to giving them.
summary: In political life and personal relationships alike, honor compensates those who contribute, while material support goes to those in need. Some debts -- to gods and parents -- can never be fully repaid, so we owe them whatever we can give. A son may not disown his father, being always in debt, but a father may forgive that debt by disowning a wicked son.
distilled: Some debts can never be fully repaid -- especially those to parents and gods -- so friendship requires not strict equivalence but the best one can give.
notes: The claim that public honor should go to public benefactors connects to the discussion of megaloprepeia (magnificence) in Book IV.2 and to the general theory of distributive justice in Book V.3. -- The asymmetry between father's right to disown and son's duty not to reflects Athenian law, where fathers had considerable legal authority (patria potestas was even more extreme in Roman law). -- The comparison of parental-filial duty to debts owed the gods connects to the broader Greek concept of piety (eusebeia) and the earlier discussion in 8.31. -- The closing observation that 'most people wish to receive benefits but avoid providing them' is characteristically Aristotelian in its sober assessment of common human motivation. -- This passage concludes Book VIII; the discussion of friendship continues directly in Book IX, which treats the dissolution of friendships, duties to different friends, and the relationship between self-love and friendship.
